Hey everyone, this is Russell. I'm so excited to have you guys here for the interview today with Armin Moran. So Armin is one of my very, very first mentors online, both from an online standpoint but also from a stage selling and speaking standpoint. He, the very first event I ever went to was Armin's events where I met John Childers where I saw this thing happening, where I saw him and other people selling and speaking and I became fascinated with it. And later he put on a training course called Persuasion X and I went through it. And I just, anyway, Armin's amazing. He's got so many good ideas but the thing that has probably made me more money than almost anything I've ever learned in the entire history of my marketing career is a concept I learned from Armin called The Stack. And we're going to talk about it and go deep in the interview. I kind of want to set it up really quick just so you guys, just so you value it as much as it's worth, right? I learned this initially. I was, I had spoken to some events. I had done well, nothing ever amazing. But then Armin invited me to speak at his event. And I was like, yeah, I would be a huge honor for you. The first event I ever went to to speak at was just crazy. And then I remember two weeks before the event happened he has spoken to the Uncanny event. I was sitting in the room and he closed almost ever in the room. I remember after I sat there watching the whole thing happen and then everyone kind of bought and they left and at the end he was standing there and I kind of walked up and I was like, dude, that was amazing. And he had in his hand the stack of order forms like this fat. He's like, you know what these are? I'm like, order forms. He's like, no, each one's a $2,000 bill. And I was like, dang. And he's like, do you want a stack of order forms like this at my event in two weeks? I'm like, yeah. He's like, all right, did you see what I did? I'm like, I saw everyone running but I don't know what you did. He's like, there's a concept in the teacher. And I was like, if you do this, you'll make more money. If you don't, you're in trouble. And I was like, hey, what is this? It's called the stack. And I'm like, what's the stack? And I'm going to briefly kind of explain it and then we'll go deeper in the interview. But he said, if you look at the way that most presenters do their presentation, right? He's like, they'll come in and they'll have their offer. Here's the thing you're going to buy. And he's like, and then I'm going to give you this bonus right here. And there's the bonus. And then I'm going to give you this bonus. And I'm going to give you this bonus. I'm going to give you this. And they go through all their stuff. He said, the human mind can only remember one thing at a time. He says, what happens is you go through all your offer. And the very last thing they're like, the total price of this is $10,000. But I'm not going to charge you 10 grand. I'm not going to charge you a thousand. And you do this whole thing. He said, the problem is the human mind, all that remembers is the very last thing you said was this right here. He said, and so then they're associating the price with the very last thing. And typically the bonuses get worse and worse and worse towards the end. So when you say it's $1,000, is this thing worth $1,000? And they're like, I don't know if it is or not. He said, that's what kills your cell. He said, what I do is I introduce the thing. So here's the bonus. And then you have a stack slice. It says what you're going to get then is you're going to get this, which the total value is this. And then the second thing you're going to get is you're going to get this thing right here. And it brings it back to the stack slice. And so what that means is you're going to get this plus this, which the total value is this. And then you're going to get this thing over here. You talk about it again. Then you come back and you stack it again. So you're going to get this and you stack it, you stack it, you stack it. And every single time you do that, so the very end, before you reveal the price, you do the big stack of there's everything you're going to get. Then you reveal the price and what happens, then they associate the price right here with everything that's happening. He's like, that right there is the secret. And I remember I was like, okay, I'm going to try that. So I put it together in my slides. I put it, you know, I did the stack the best I could, just like he taught me. And two weeks later at his event and I spoke and it was the first time I'd broken six figures. I think it was like $180,000. So I ended up walking out with a check. My portion of like $90,000. And one of my friends who was there at the time, who, who, uh, Brent Co-Peters, one of my partners in ClickFunnels now is the first time he ever came to the event. And we got it on the stage. He had the stack, my stack of order forms. And he's like, do you realize he's like, you just netted $90,000 in an hour and a half. He's like, most people will work for three or four years to get that. Um, and you did it in an hour. And I was just like, yeah, that was amazing. And I remember this distinct feeling that I remember after learning this, I went back to my next presentation and I remember as I was doing this first time, I felt kind of cheesy and weird. I'm like, I don't know if I like doing, I'm not going to do it again. And I didn't and myself tank. And then I was like, I'm going to plug it back in and my cells went up. So I started doing it a bunch of times again. And I transitioned to webinars. And this again, two years later, I transitioned to webinars. And I remember, um, I did it and I was like, I feel cheesy doing this. I stopped. I was like, I'm not going to stack anymore. And I stopped and my cells like dried up to nothing. I did two or three webinars with no stack and I was like, gosh, dang it. I'm going to put it back in. So I put it back in. Next time I put it in, it just crushed again. I was like, okay, note to self. I will never give a praise presentation again ever in the history of my life without using the stack. And I haven't. If you ever see me speak or sell anything, I always use stack every single time because it works. And so if nothing else, you learn from this entire training program, the stack will make you more money. I see people using this now on Facebook lives. I see them using it in their emails. I'm just using it on the sales. There's video sales. There's like any spot that you sell ever, the stack is the key. Okay. This is worth the price of admission by itself. And I'm excited to interview Armand having to go deep into the stack and how it works and why it works and how he figured it out. Um, but I promise you guys that this, this is the secret right here. Um, another part of the bonuses you'll see down below in the, in the, the section here is, um, I actually taught my stack, how I do it, um, at one of our fat events. And so I'm going to include that video down below as well. So you can actually see me doing my stack. And I, I'll kind of go in state where I'll like, I'll do the stack and I'll pause it and I'll come back and explain what I did. And I'll go back and forth and back and forth. So you see me doing mine. Um, but the stack is the key. Um, just do it. I promise you it's worth it. You may feel a little weird the first or second time, but we start caching the big checks that would be worth it every single time. So with that said, let's jump in the interview with Armand. I mean, it's crazy, but, but again, it comes back to this one piece and it's one thing called the stack. And, uh, it's something I developed a long time ago because just like Russell said, there's a lot of great people that he learned from as well. And I learned from the, some of those same, same people, John Childers, you know, probably the only person in the world that I can call a mentor of mine. He actually taught me everything I need to know about speaking, what to say, how to say it, but there was one part that no matter how great of a speaker John was, he, he just never conveyed to me exactly how to close or close properly. And the, I kept watching people doing different kinds of presentations and presentations and, and way back when, again, going back far enough, all these presentations were like super long and it wasn't just their presentation. It was their clothes. When the part where they told everyone exactly what they should buy or how they should buy, and it was just long, long, long, long. And by the time you got to the end of that presentation, people forget what they're actually buying because you got this bonus and this bonus and this bonus. And by the time you finished, there was a problem. And that's the first thing I realized. And I think a lot of times when we're actually looking at things, when we're looking at someone else's presentation, we can see the mistakes. And for me, it was that the presentation, the close was too long and people just forgot what they were buying. So I said, well, what if we did something a little bit different? And it was this idea that I had and just kind of this glimpse of a moment. You know, sometimes you have that one little tiny moment of clarity. And I said, well, why don't we just kind of like itemize it? Kind of like a little bit of a list, like a checklist. This is, you're going to get this and you're going to get this. And then a little bit later, add a few more things and then remind them. So it's like a little reminder the whole way is like, and you'll get this and you'll get this and you get this. And, and then a little bit more, you add a little bit more to it. And then you total everything up again, you're going to get this, this, this, this, and this. And then at the end, you do this big kind of crescendo, kind of like classical music, where you're going to get this and this and this and this and this. And then you're going to get all these different things and people see the total value. And I think that's what the problem was back then is that people were just missing the total value. Does that, does that make sense, Russell? Yeah, because you're right. You see people need to close for 30 minutes and they've mentioned like 20 things, but our minds remember like one or two, not like all of it. And so they, yeah, um, a hundred percent. Is that it seems like you're repeating yourself again and again and again. But what I think people forget is that what, what the people in the audience, it's not as boring cause they don't know what's coming up. And what you're doing is you're kind of doing them a service by reminding them. These are all the different things that you're getting. Again, this is why you want my package. And again, they don't know how much it is, or they don't know what it is. They don't know the total totality of everything that you're offering them. See, here's the thing when speaking is that if people don't see the value that they're getting and what you're offering, they're not going to buy it. One person stood up and said, I'm not going to do those things because where you give this big price and you then, you know, drop and drop and drop and drop. And you know, I just don't want to play those games. I just want to give people a price. And, and here's my answer. I am just going to give them the price and what it is because my products are so good, they stand on their own and they sell themselves. And that's what they say. And they're always the worst selling speaker on that stage. Always. You've seen it. Am I right? Uh-huh. I, and if I always go back, cause I actually did it one time, I, I did the same thing. I was like, I remember saying, I'm not going to insult your intelligence, like the other speakers in two, I was like, I sold zero and I was like, I think I just insulted my own intelligence. Like, I didn't do it. But what, but here's the thing is it's the same reason why stores have discounts and sales that going on is people want to actually feel like they're getting a deal. They want to feel special. They're buying the package because not only are you speaking and sharing your knowledge with those people, hopefully throughout your presentation, but at the same time, what you're selling them is you're, you're so you're entertaining them. You're, you're selling you and closing doesn't have to be this nervous process. I mean, for that much, because those are all that your mother told you not to do. But that is how we get paid as a speaker presenting products and services from the stage. So with the stack, what it does is it evens the playing field. I have, I'm going to tell you, I have lost my whole presentations at times where it was, I was dying. And here's kind of what you want to think about is that in the very beginning, the audience is actually a little nervous. They don't know who you are. They have this opinion of you possibly they don't know what to expect. So you want to start off kind of slow and in, even in my pace of speech. So the way I'm speaking to you right now is even a little bit faster than what I would normally start speaking at because I want to get these people comfortable with me. And the more I go on throughout the presentation, my pattern of speech gets a little bit faster. Now Russell, he has one speed. He just speaks fast. If you could only slow down a little bit, he would be awesome. But now what happens is what happens is your, your pace and your presentation starts kind of slow. And then towards the end, it gets really, really fast towards the end because what we're doing is we're creating a sense of excitement and the things that people associate with excitement is speaking a little bit faster than you normally do speaking a little bit louder than you normally do. Um, I want everyone to write down, if you're taking notes, I want you to write down this word, it's called the segue. Okay. The segue is where you transition from the actual teaching to the actual closing. And the way that you do that is you simply ask a question and typically is who would like to be able to do the things I just taught them basically in one way, shape or form. So for example, I might say who wants to make, uh, X number of dollars by selling this product or service or whatever it may be that I'm teaching in this case happens to be doing YouTube videos. So in this case, I tell, I talk about who wants Oprah money. And then I show them a picture of Oprah. Right. And it goes through this whole thing where you get a car, you get a car, you get a car, right? So I kind of take them because I, cause here's the thing, here's the thing I want everyone to understand is that it's funny, right? You have to entertain as you're going through this. So there's some things that I just think are funny. And when Oprah does that, I think it's funny. And that's why I created this brand new program. And then I tell them what it is called two bunch manure, right? So then, um, I go through the presentation and I tell them what it is now. It's very simple as far as how this actually works. Okay. Um, we tell them, I do some things to cut them up ahead of time. Who's this program for, right? That's the first part. And then I tell them, uh, what you're going to receive is this, you're going to get instant access to the system. And I'm going to tell you what you're going to learn. And so I walk them through what the course that I'm selling is all about. Okay. Um, here's one of the keys. Okay. I want you to take this, this down because one of the things that I give everyone, and this is the biggest part of my closing piece, then why people buy is lifetime access to my course. So when I sell them a course, I give them lifetime access. Why? Because it has a huge, huge, massive value. Now there's been times where I've been on stage and probably Russell has as well, where someone will say something like, and I'm going to give you six months of customer support, absolutely for free. I'm like, really? Really? That seems kind of straight. So what you, if you give them lifetime access, it doesn't cost you anything. That's the beautiful part about this. And so what you're doing is instead of going, you're creating the distance from the bottom, what most people do by reducing the price, we're actually increasing the value and creating the contrast on the top side rather than the bottom side. Um, you know what I think it is, is just it's the package. Think of your close or your ending of your presentation. As, as a package, you're packaging up your offer. You can have the greatest product in the world, but if it's not packaged properly, then what's happened is people are not going to, going to see it. Um, they're not going to see it at all. Mentioning or motioning for them to do that. Yeah. It does take some, you know, Cajones, right? To say some of these things. And yes, it is going to feel uncomfortable. Yes, it's going to be, it's, it's, it's not natural. I can tell you that. But, you know, I'll tell everyone this is that, you know, I had to learn everything that I did. I'm a, I know some people don't think this, but I'm technically a very shy individual most of the time, except when I go up and speak in front of a stage in front of thousands of people or something like that. But I had to learn all those things because I was so shy when I first started my, uh, in life, literally when I was 18, 19 years old, I would actually cross the street if someone was coming down on the same side of the street as me. But I also know that when you walk up on stage, that people have a certain level of expectation from you. And so what you need to do is you need to turn that switch on and you need to be on for that 90 minutes. I think anyone can focus for 90 minutes, especially about talking about something that you know already your topic, whatever that topic or happens to be. You know that topic inside now, hopefully if you're a speaker and then the packaging it together at the end and wrapping it up in a nice bow on top of it for someone to buy, just making it easy. Because what we're trying to do is we're trying to remove that barrier to entry that people have. And so we give them a price and we make us, we make them feel good about it by reducing it down and dropping in various ways to do that. And then at the end coming in at the last part, and this is the part where that last drop that I will take, I will finance you, I will take payments from you. That last piece was the secret, was one of the coolest things I ever did. Because honestly, it made a lot of people mad over the years. And the reason is, is because at once I could have been the highest price in the room. I could have been, everyone else in the room could have been $1,500 or $2,000. Right. And my product might have been $2,000 as well. But if I break it down to payments, it's only like $7.76. You see what I mean? So now I'm the lowest price in the room. And if they don't take payments, then, then they're not going to get the sale. I'm going to get the sale. And so just by breaking it up into payments, if you're willing to take payments, which everyone should be willing to do, then it's no problem except only three payments when you're selling on stage. Yeah. So it's just, it's amazing. And I think, you know, the very, again, I told you just in the very beginning, the very first time I came to the big seminar, I think it was like three or four or something, the very, when the first one, the first one I went to, and I saw it. And I was the shy, awkward, introverted kid who didn't dare talk to anyone. I remember after I saw that, I was like, I have to learn the skill. It's like all these little tiny things. And it's like, sometimes I'll tell you what, because especially if you've done it for a number of years and spoken a lot of different events, you actually forget some of the stuff that actually worked really well. And you have to kind of keep coming back to it. So I actually had to do, you know, actually had to do that recently is that because my presentations have changed a little bit over the years. I had to figure out ways to change it for specific audiences. And every time you make a change, it tweaks it just a little bit. So if you are modifying or tweaking a presentation over the course of multiple years, what you started with and what you ended with could be two different things. But what you ended with may not be working. So you have to kind of go in the beginning. So actually went back to all my presentations for about 10 years back. And I'm like, okay, I know this presentation work because I did like $600,000 sales with this, right? And, you know, 90 minute presentations. I'm like, this one works. So let's figure out what changed between here and there. And honestly, it was like these tiny little things that I took out because I was doing a webinar or I had to cut time off of it or something else. And in the end, I'm like, I had this presentation over here, which said the same things, but it wasn't right. So I went back and had to regroup again and it worked. You know, it just, you know, had to think about it a little bit, but you tend to forget things or you see something that worked or you tried something and it's just all these different things, but you have to keep learning. And I think that's kind of the key part is you can't assume that just because you've been doing this for a long time, that it's always going to work because it doesn't. I mean, I've gone, you know, I've bottomed, yeah, I've bombed in the past, just like everyone else has, but, you know, I've bombed a lot less now than when I once did.
